ocar.opportunity: Local connectome reconstructions of brain sub-volumes provided rich circuit detail but were insufficient for understanding global information flow, sensorimotor integration, and whole-brain computation in any organism.
ocar.challenge: Reconstructing 139,255 neurons and 50 million synapses from a complete adult fly brain required new scalable segmentation pipelines, extensive distributed proofreading, and interoperable data infrastructure capable of handling petabyte-scale EM volumes.
ocar.action: The authors generated a complete adult female Drosophila melanogaster whole-brain connectome using FIB-SEM imaging combined with flood-filling network segmentation and community proofreading. They annotated cell classes, types, hemilineages, and predicted neurotransmitter identities for all neurons, then analyzed projectome structure and information flow from sensory inputs to motor outputs.
ocar.resolution: The FlyWire connectome provides a neuronal wiring diagram with 139,255 neurons, 50 million synapses, and comprehensive cell-type annotations, publicly accessible for download and interactive browsing. Projectome analysis and pathway tracing from photoreceptors to descending motor neurons demonstrate how the connectome enables mechanistic hypotheses about sensorimotor circuits.
ocar.future_work: Extending this approach to other Drosophila individuals to assess inter-individual variability, and applying similar methods to other species, are natural next steps.

plain_language_summary: This paper produced the first complete wiring diagram of an entire adult fruit fly brain, mapping over 139,000 neurons and 50 million connections. Think of it as producing a full circuit schematic for a tiny but sophisticated computer — one that processes sight, smell, and touch, then decides how to move. The data are public and the team built tools so any researcher can browse and download the circuit of interest.

summaries.beginner: Neurons in a brain talk to each other through connections called synapses. This paper used an electron microscope to image an entire adult fly brain, then used machine learning to identify every neuron and trace every connection — over 50 million of them. The resulting map (the FlyWire connectome) is freely available, letting scientists look up how sensory information travels all the way to movement commands.
summaries.intermediate: Dorkenwald et al. present the FlyWire whole-brain connectome of adult D. melanogaster, achieving 139,255 reconstructed neurons and ~5×10^7 chemical synapses via flood-filling network segmentation on FIB-SEM data with distributed community proofreading. The dataset includes predicted neurotransmitter identities, hemilineage annotations, and a derived projectome. Pathway tracing from photoreceptors through the visual system to descending motor neurons illustrates the resource's utility for mechanistic circuit analysis.
summaries.advanced: FlyWire represents a qualitative advance over the hemibrain dataset (Scheffer et al. 2020) by covering both hemispheres and connecting central brain to optic lobes. The segmentation pipeline used flood-filling networks with iterative agglomeration; proofreading quality is quantified by false-merge and split rates. Key limitations include single-animal reconstruction, potential segmentation errors in dense neuropil, and the ongoing challenge of matching morphologically defined cell types to genetic lines. The open-ecosystem approach with API access, CAVE backend, and interoperability with other fly resources (neuPrint, VFB) sets a standard for future large-scale connectomics.

discussion_prompts: FlyWire uses community proofreading by many annotators — what quality-control measures were employed, and how do residual errors propagate into circuit-level analyses?
The paper derives a 'projectome' from the connectome — what additional information does this region-to-region connectivity matrix provide compared to individual neuron-level wiring?
How would you use the FlyWire connectome to generate and test a specific hypothesis about a sensorimotor behavior in Drosophila?
